transcript.transcript: All right, it is May 30th, 2023, and I'm conducting an oral history with Mr. David Mann in his home in Bloomfield. David, thank you so much for doing this. I really appreciate you taking the time. If you could start thank you so much for I really appreciate you taking the time. If you for doing this. the time. If you could start by telling me a little bit about your background. Okay. Yeah, Yeah, that's a certainly a leading question. Where do you want to start? Tell me a little bit about your family background as far back as you can go, your maternal and then your paternal. Okay. I was born in 1941, and the parents who were second-generation Americans, their parents migrated from Russia and Germany, and we grew up in the city of Patterson, New Jersey, where my father's six siblings all lived at the time. It was an environment in which there was close involvement with family every Sunday at grandma's kind of a thing. Of course, this was well before TV or other kinds of technologies that would have taken us away from each other. Family was important. Of course, you thought your family was the best in the world until you grew up and realized that maybe they weren't so great, or they were different, but you bought it, and it was reinforced throughout the whole community. We were Jewish people. My father sang in the choral society at Temple Emmanuel, which I joined him many years later doing the same. We would go to Hebrew school two, three times a week. It wasn't such a burden, although I'd much rather have been playing baseball, but most of of the people in my neighborhood went, or many of my friends went. It It was a self-reinforcing, almost sort of a ghetto consciousness that you go because the because the others are there, and that's the lifestyle. To start things off a little bit differently, my father was working as a manager at a smoked fish factory. at a smoked fish factory. Eventually, he started his own business as a news dealer, but before that, he took a job in Meridian, Mississippi during the war as a manager of a trucking terminal. I never realized how prescient that was because it was in Meridian, Mississippi where he met the parents of my to-be-bride, who wasn't even born then. It was interesting that we grew up in a community where there were mostly white people in our part of town. He found himself working in Meridian, Mississippi, and from his experiences, I got a good sense of his appreciation for people of all peoples. He certainly didn't come back from Mississippi with any kind of hate or disregard. In In fact, he came back with the opposite. How long was he in Mississippi? I I think we were there for two years. Of course, I was a baby, so I don't remember too much. What time period? This This is 1941 to 1943. I came back to Patterson and grew up going to grammar schools, doing all those things. I I was mostly interested in sports and athletics. I I can't say I was a great student. I wasn't bad, but I wasn't a great student. At that age, it just wasn't motivating. What was motivating was being outdoors. Of course, in those days, being outdoors meant after breakfast, you were gone until you were hungry and you came back and you go back out again. It was that kind of parenting and that kind of experiencing. You socialized yourself. You had difficult people to deal with. You either learned how to deal with them or you didn't. You didn't look to your mother or your father to take care of those issues. It was a simple life. You didn't have much. You made as much out of what you did have or your own thoughts as possible. Growing up, I thought, was really wonderful. There was no pressure and little expectations other than, again, showing up for dinner on time. Can you say what drew your parents, your family, to Patterson? Well, I think my father was born there. His parents were there already. My mother was born maybe 20 miles outside of Patterson. Her family eventually moved to Patterson as well. Patterson is a community very much like Hartford, both in size and industrial quality and age. There's a lot of other things I could tell you about that are comparable, but it's a little off the subject. Growing up, my first experiences outside the hood were when I had to go to high school. This was at a time when it was just about 1954 when suburban development just started happening. My parents, like many parents at the time, sought to buy their first house. As the economy was expanding and housing was available, they bought the house in the neighboring community of Fairlawn, a suburb, and moved there. What kind of housing was your family living in before? Two- or three-family housing. That was predominant in the neighborhood I was in. There were other neighborhoods where there was a single family, but this was their first foray into owning their own home. Before Fairlawn, you were living in a two- or three-family, but you're renting? Yes, renters. Are they renting from another Jewish family? Yeah, it was renting from a guy who lived two houses away who didn't like kids playing and making noise. How did you find that out? Because he would come when we were playing in the driveway and say, Boydice, get out of here! This no place for play! Those kinds of things you never forget. But he was an old man and you dealt with him as you needed to. I don't know how my parents did, but that's their business. It was never really serious. Just a curmudgeonly man. Yeah. Yeah. What was the street like? Can you tell me a little bit about the street vibe, the families on the street? Well, it was mixed. My closest friends were young Italian fellows. There were There were several others who were not Jewish in the neighborhood. There were times when you heard detestable things said about you being Jewish. There were times when you didn't think about it. But there was a time in the 40s, there was still a lot of serious anti-Semitism in this country, and we were brought up to keep our heads low, head down, so to speak, so as not to be visible in a way. Did your parents deliberately tell you that? They gave us a little bit of direction, sort of like the way black people tell their kids about what they need to know. Of course, the previous years, the reasons the U.S. didn't get into the war, there were a lot of people who were just anti-Semitic. That was something that was in our minds all day, all the time. When we went out of the neighborhood, even in Hebrew school, they wouldn't teach you that. That wasn't part of what they were teaching. They were simply teaching the Hebrew language in order to be bar mitzvahed and how to read it. Not necessarily how to speak it, because that wasn't an official language until 1948, when Israel was created. How did anti-Semitism show up in your high school? Not blatantly. I don't recall it being visible in high school amongst kids our age. Of course, in high school, when that time was in Fairlawn, the surrounding town, a a lot of Jewish people moved there from Patterson as well. It was just something we were told about by our parents. Moving on. As you're transitioning to high school, what what was your sense of the world? Pretty limited. I was, again, interested pretty much in sports. We would have our fun games. The Kikes would play the Wops. We used those names. Who came up with those names? The players themselves. With a laugh and a smile. We never took it seriously as being a nasty pejorative, in a sense. But we would play intramural. I played varsity ball and enjoyed that. I was more interested in that than most other things. Did you say varsity? Baseball and soccer. First-year soccer team, because we didn't know what soccer was back then. I didn't know either, but I figured it was better than getting my butt kicked playing football, because I didn't know where I wanted to play there either. What's the typical class background of people who are living in Fairlawn? That varies. I think there were some garden apartments in the town. Most of the town was small, single-family, postwar housing. They might be considered middle class by that sense. Of course, during those years, you didn't know what upper class was. You never saw them. There There weren't that many, and you didn't know. Around what year did your dad, did you guys get the house in Fairlawn? About 1957. By that time, what kind of job was your father doing? He had owned a business in Ridgewood, New Jersey, distributing newspapers. The way it worked, of course, the New York metropolitan area had 15 or so newspapers. Each newspaper did not deliver their own papers. They would rely on distributors to take them and deliver them to other people, who, like my father, would hire local kids and deliver door-to-door. Were you a newspaper? At age 12, I was conscripted to work on Sunday mornings, which meant I'd have to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning and go in. At that point, I would just take a nap in the car while he was organizing stuff. But this was a wonderful experience for me, because most of the people he employed were football players and athletes from the town of Ridgewood. I don't know how he got them, but a lot of them came forward, and it was a good exercise. You run with an armful of papers. It gave me a good sense of responsibility, so much so that by age 17, I was almost managing the place. Eventually, my father sold it to somebody else, and I stayed with that guy for a few years to keep him going. I had a few jobs. I worked in an auto supply store doing sales, but also working with people who have questions about what's wrong with their TV. I would look up in the book. I figured I'd test a few tubes, because back then all TVs had tubes. Each tube had a function, and if something was going wrong, it was likely a tube. What kind of store was it? Strauss Auto Stores. Other than working with my dad, I also worked in the post office for two years. Everybody had a job. Let me clarify, because you said auto, but then people are bringing in... It's auto supply, and it's one of those little shops that have a little of everything. And so you repair... I was mostly working, I guess, stock, keeping the stock current, keeping the place clean, and also learning to handle these phone calls about TV repairs. So TV's there by then? Did you have any particular shows that you liked to watch? Oh, back in the day? Saturday morning as a kid was fun, because it was all cartoons. I enjoyed the comedy shows, and Elton Burroughs and the others, by and large, because most of them contained comedians who were of my ilk. I mean, not my ilk, but they're Jewish comedians. I learned a lot about who those other people are out there. But, you know, there were some good science shows and so forth, but nothing like the programming today. In fact, it wasn't sufficient enough to keep me from going out every day and playing, or the weekends. And what did your friendship network look like in high school? Well, I joined the high school fraternity, which... you know, looking back on it, I wasn't sure it had any social purpose and not outside the group, you know, no community function other than have a good time. But it was one of those things that my brother had been in, and I tried to follow suit and see what that was like. It was really not as exciting as my college fraternity experience. What was the name of the high school fraternity? Kappa Alpha Tau. Cats. We were the cats, man. Kappa Alpha Tau. Okay. And so... Let's back up a little bit to your siblings. I have a brother who's seven years older. Thanks God he's still alive. He's going to be 89 this year. And he's probably the most interesting guy I ever got to spend time with. Where is he? He's in New Jersey. He never left? He never left New Jersey. He left Patterson, sure. He also has homes in Long Island and Malibu and other places, but he's really made it. And he made it on his own ability. He was more of a depression baby than I. I I. I wasn't a depression baby. So he probably had a higher expectation of what he wanted after that. He was a designer. What kind of designer? He was an industrial designer. And he'd done very well doing that until he realized that his consultancy, he'd be better off going into manufacturing because because he's just giving everybody else the directions of what to do. And he did very well with that. And it enabled him to do what he really wanted to do, which is to get into classic car acquisitions and showing. He probably has one of the largest, best collections on the East Coast of cars, many of which are 1930s French and other designs. And he's very successful at Pebble Beach in California doing their show. So he did very well, and as you can imagine, here I am seven years later and not having all that close a relationship because by the time I was in grammar school, he was in high school and almost ready to leave. So we really didn't have that kind of day-to-day interactions that you'd think close brothers would. But I certainly respect him and still love him. We still have something going. Of course, looking at his level of success, I looked at it, but it wasn't necessarily what I'm looking for. So you had no interest in him? I didn't. I mean, I must have had some psychological issues growing up. I mean, he was doing so well. I wasn't doing badly, but I wasn't in there to make money. It was never my goal. I just wanted to have something that encouraged me and gave me a decent life. And I found that in college. All right. So when you were thinking about going to college, what did you set your sights? How far did you set your sights? I didn't. There were no such thing. Why was I going to college? Because everybody in the neighborhood was going to college. It was group motivation. Did I have a specific goal? No. My parents thought it would be good to be a lawyer or something like that. One of my uncles was a lawyer, and everybody respected him. All right. I went to Rutgers thinking maybe I'll tee myself up in that area. I was a political science major, but I didn't really care for the dusty books by reading about political science. It didn't motivate me a whole lot at the time, although they were interesting, and I thought this is where I was going. I was much more affected by music and art history as well as Western civilization history to understand how humans function and over time and so forth that eventually led me to city planning, which was a minor offered at the time that pulled together all of these different subject matters as to how humans cohabitate, how they function, what kind of infrastructure is required for them to function, how do you even think about future needs, and how do you do assessments to determine how much money you need to do different projects or budget for something for a long-term gain. It was very exciting because you put yourself right in the center of everything that would be happening. So you did political science and urban planning? Yes. What was going on with the field of urban planning in general? Was it relatively new? Oh, it was very new. new? Oh, it was very new. There had been some examples, one in my own community in Fairlawn, of people, I'm trying to think if I remember the fellow's name, an English fellow who had created this little garden city in Radburn. It was the first development where cars didn't go all the way through it. It was more for pedestrians. Cars went under the road. It was set up for cars but not conflicting with residents. It was It was kind of like a garden city concept. So that was in my own town, so I had a chance to look at something that's different than the normal subdivision and think about that, have something to say about that. But college was good for that purpose, figuring out what I wanted to do by the third year in college, and then everything took off from there. One of the highlights I just mentioned to you is my fraternity experience. I chose to pledge a fraternity that was a diverse group of people and ethnicities and so forth. It was rare at the time in the early 60s. But it was extremely rewarding. What brought you to it? Who brought you to it? I it? I was rushed. As an athlete, their primary purpose is to improve their intramural teams for sure. But one fellow who actually was dating some girl that I went to grammar school with, and he got my name. But the group was interesting because, again, they're from all over the country. You learn to live with people and how to socialize. Everybody's got issues just like you do. But it really was an eye-opener for me with respect to actually living with people I hadn't lived with before. Did you guys live in a frat house? Yeah, yeah, with a house mother who was half-deaf and really old. Probably that was a necessary criteria for the job. But that was a good experience, wonderful experience for me. Was that junior year and senior year? It was sophomore, junior, and senior year. So three years of sharing those kinds of close-up experiences with people, nothing like anything before then. Did you leave the state by then to travel for vacation? Not really. My first time I left the state was after graduation. I took a job in Tennessee. I had been working while I was at Rutgers in Morristown for the county planning board just to see if this is what I really wanted. But I still wasn't sure how much more I needed to invest in it. So I took a job in Nashville. I was attracted to Nashville for a couple of reasons. One is that I had an aunt and uncle that lived there. Two is that they had just gone and created a metropolitan government and took all the smaller communities within the county and made one government. However much I got out of the political science background, that was amazing to think that there could be such consolidation. If you think about it in terms of the 169 towns in Connecticut, everybody has to have their own this and their own that, school boards, police boards, fire boards, everything is so inefficient. In a small state like this. So I was on to this early to see how this was going. So I spent two years there in the planning department. Got to have a good experience there. So much so that I was able to then say, yeah, this is what I really want. But if I'm going to do it, I want to get a master's degree. How is the field of urban planning emerging by the time you're in this job in Tennessee? That's a good question. It was emerging very strongly primarily because of the views of John Kennedy, President Kennedy, who created a whole new aura of excitement and expectation about doing public work, public service. Ask not what society can do for you, but what you can do for them. So there was a whole renaissance of public service. People wanted to go into local governments. Of Of course, this was a time of expansion, too, because there was a lot of post-war development going on. So governments were in need of expansion. And so I was lucky enough to be selected out of 450 applicants to be part of a 45-group class at MIT. And I was blown away. I just couldn't believe, A, I got to go to a place like that, and, B, it was just so stimulating no matter where you went. Even Even if you weren't in the building, you're close to Harvard, you're at Boston. I mean, I mean, it was just a wonderful environment. You put your head straight and you figure, okay, I'm going to just do what I've got to do here. What would have been the other options? What other programs were there that were competitive with MIT at the time? I think I got into Cornell and one other, but I can't remember. I think that's about it. But the professors at MIT were world-renowned, and I was so happy to be there and to learn from them, be part of that process. So eventually I got my degree. I was focusing on regional economics because of one professor, Lloyd Rodwin, who also was doing international development in developing countries. I was into urban design because the fellow who almost created the subject was teaching there. But then I was also very much impacted by advocacy planning, by people who worked in neighborhoods where the rubber meets the road. So when it came time to graduate, two things happened. One is I got married to Sharon, who I had met while I was in Nashville. The other thing was I took a job with a group of professors working in Puerto Rico on an economic development study. A regional study trying to see how they can best decentralize the oil industry, which was creating a great deal of urban pressure in a few cities. And then after that, which was a short stint to do that job, both of us packed up a car and moved to Cambridge. A short stint, three months, summer? It was maybe six months. Let's see, we went back to Cambridge. Oh, we were in Cambridge to do that job, that's right. We got married and went to Cambridge, back to Cambridge to do that job. And then I'm looking after that as to where I'm going to go from here. So I applied three places. One was in Stanford, Connecticut, which at that time was a real hole in the wall. Why did you want to go there? Well, it was because they had a position. And it was in Connecticut and it wasn't too far, so I checked it. The other was Shaw neighborhood of Washington, D.C. And this would have been, I would have probably been the only white person on the staff in that location. I was ready for it, but they weren't at the time. But it would have been more of the advocacy planning, working for the people. And I came to Hartford because I found I could do the same thing here, and also because I had previous experience in Hartford. I'd been here as a kid. I had another aunt and uncle who lived in Hartford. And I noticed looking back at my history, I do follow known trails. I'm not necessarily a trailblazer to find new territory, but places I've been before. But the attraction of Hartford was tremendous corporate involvement at the time. A beautiful town with nice memories for me as growing up. And I would have been heading up a neighborhood planning effort in Clay Hill, South Arsenal, Upper Albany. And that was— Did you have any particular programs at Model Cities? Well, when Model Cities came out, I was interim director until they would get somebody to full-time. But beyond that, we set up a number of programs using federal monies, neighborhood development planning. There were a couple of CDAPs. I don't remember what that means anymore. But Connecticut had their own money. But I was working with residents in the Clay Hill neighborhood. And we had worked together. They claimed there was not enough good space for the kids, indoors for recreation, no recreation. So I was able to secure a building from Aetna that the town had purchased in order to build the Quirk Middle School. But it was going to take a while for that to get done. So we were raising money to outfit the building. And wouldn't you know it, that's when the riots occurred. And that building was hit amongst other buildings. So that went by the boards. How far along was the building? This was an older building that was going to come down in about two years. So it would have given them an opportunity then that some of that activity could have gone into the school. So, you know, it was always a difficult task trying to estimate— or not estimate, to hear what the residents are saying. And trying to bring something back to City Hall and say, we need to do something, here's some ideas that I think would be helpful in this neighborhood. To what do you attribute that difficulty? Well, for one thing, there's not a blank check. There's—anything you bring in has to sort of get in line and get on the— it can't bypass stuff that's already being planned, even though it may be more valuable. But at one point, the Chamber of Commerce wanted to do some apartment renovations in that area. And I said, well, that's great, but you've got to provide adequate off-street parking and and recreation space for the people there. You can't just do the buildings. Well, that sort of got me a little bit of heat with the town officials, but they did it. It was done. Where were these apartments that were renovated? Bedford Street. So after a few years in planning, I was attracted to a job in redevelopment. I I became the Deputy Director of Redevelopment. The Redevelopment Agency? Yeah. And wrote about six redevelopment plans for areas, including the old Underwood area, Charter Oak, Congress Street, and several—a few at downtown. Kind of things that I was really happy with. I mean, before that, I had published through the Planning Department neighborhood analysis of Hartford and and what the changes are, what are some areas that need to be attended to. I did a plan for downtown Hartford, which eventually became utilized to create zoning bonuses for things of public need. We eventually established a design review board so that that public need could be more directly carried out by a group of people who could have discussions of those amenities and those improvements, apolitical. In Hartford, the council is a zoning board. The city council is a zoning board? So, you know, there's a lot of details of it. I don't know where we want to go. So when you first started in Hartford in terms of urban planning, was there a lot of— what was the energy around model cities and these other kinds of programs? Well, there was like almost a different program every six months. This was during the Democratic reign, Kennedy, Johnson, so forth, and they were coming out with all kinds of new society, new ventures to try and deal with local problems. In retrospect, all those were exciting new innovations. No one had any idea how they were going to turn out. No one had any idea whether they were going to continue to be funded. And all it did in many cases is just create a lot more frustration. Because of the promises? Yeah, a lot of promises that I wouldn't say were kept, but just didn't develop. For example, I was working on—well, Hartford created the Constitution Plaza, which was the national test for constitutionality of MN Domain, by the way. And it was a good concept. The idea being that that neighborhood, which existed before then, which was extremely vibrant and extremely exciting, but which had fallen apart over years of flooding before the dikes were built, and a time when people had other options to move out of there. And the idea being that you wanted to reclaim the mistakes of the highway by the river and get to the river. Can we start over? Yes. Because I'm going to have to take the phone out. Oh. So, you said, Oh. take the phone out. So, you said, let's start at Constitution Plaza. Constitution Plaza. Constitution Plaza was a project that was based on an economic development concept. It also had a lot of physical development benefits to organize Hartford. However, after it was completed, that first phase was completed, and the second phase, which was to carry it to Main Street, to connect it to town, city, was lost. Because by that time, the country had moved on to other things, like low-cost housing, neighborhood housing developments. So, the idea of starting an economic policy or working with that was curtailed. It still hasn't been completed, and now it's 50 years later. So, it's standing out there in the plaza like a sore thumb in many ways. It was partially integrated by the overhead pathways that were created, but not like what was envisioned. So, each one of these projects, including... Well, let's go back to the other. So, then the movement went to some of the public housing projects. And that was good, but it was based on something I still couldn't understand. They understand. They were going to sell tax losses, people's tax losses, in order to build low-income housing. Now, Now, that was, to me, I never could figure that out. But out. But anyway, we did build a number of projects. I would call them projects, but it wasn't the kind of thing that people referred to as a project, like a public housing project. And I got involved with the public housing people, trying to do scattered site housing of smaller scale. So, trying to be very much aware of the history of the public housing movement. By the time I was involved, it clearly was on its way out. It just did not work anymore. So, how is it different from the earlier era of wartime and then transitional housing in the 40s that brought Charter Oak and Bellevue Square? Well, that's an interesting question. I'm not sure I can answer it fully, but I remember growing up in a neighborhood in Patterson. They built a public housing project a half a block away. It was mostly for veterans. Right. So, you maybe had the thought that it's not permanent. It's a way station or something. But public housing was fine for a number of years until it wasn't. And And I don't know whether it was either poor management that eventually did it in or higher expectations. I mean, there were some really wonderful people working at Hartford Housing Authority when I was there. They were trying to do the best that they could. But there just wasn't enough property. The properties were not meant to last. They were built for short-term expectations, sort of like those veteran housings. So, what was the thinking behind trying to resolve housing problems in Hartford then? The timeline that I have is that their visions of the city being renovated, especially especially what commercial business district, into the 1930s with these grand aspirations about removing slums and the whole slum clearance plan created for Hartford by 1934. And then 1936 and 1938 and the floods kind of delayed those plans a bit but bit but also set other plans in motion for public housing because all of these people who were now made even more vulnerable because of the hurricanes needed needed a place to live quickly alongside the coming of the war. So, that covers the war. So, that covers the 1930s and the 1940s, almost a vision whose time had come but hurried along by the floods and hurried along by the needs for wartime housing. So, So, I'm sort of wondering how does all that sediment kind of wash out by the time you're around? Well, one thing that was made pretty apparent is the impact of industrial employment in Hartford, the reduction in industrial employment. After the war, a lot of these plants went elsewhere. Hartford was an amazing industrial component to its history and it, like most other places, changed when transportation modes were improved and others opening up other areas for modernization. While many of them left Hartford, a lot of the residents went with them too. A lot of the residents had other housing opportunities elsewhere as subdivision development took place, which, by the way, I have a historic feeling that it just didn't create the kind of neighborhood, the kind of living environment that was good for people. It's too isolating. But that wasn't the feeling at the time. No, No, it wasn't the feeling. The car was going to change. Right. The car was going to make it possible for you to go wherever you needed to go. Absolutely. go. Absolutely. And everybody bought into it. Everybody. It also created a great deal more open space and buildings, rentals. There There was a lot of opportunity for people to move into Hartford. Early 50s, I think, a lot of the people working in the tobacco industry migrated to Hartford. That was the only place that had multifamily housing that could accommodate. That industry kind of petered out. I don't know if people stayed there or not. I don't know. But it provided a little bit of a cushion for the next ten years. You mean in terms of the industrial? In terms of housing availability. There was housing availability. There was housing available. By By the time I got there in 1968, there was not a lot of housing available. But the population still was down tens of thousands from what it was in the beginning of the century. So it became— Were people frustrated by then? On On the developer side. Because there was a lot of optimism at the beginning of Constitution Plaza planning. I've seen the plans and read some of the oral histories associated with it. But by the time the project actually finished— Well, I think, yes, there was some frustration. There There was a market breakdown. When you couldn't continue the momentum, you were shunted to do different projects in different places. None of which had necessarily an economic impact other than because there was housing. It It was a housing impact, no question. And you were creating little communities with people in the co-ops. But there were associations involved. You must have done about three or four of them in the center of town as well as on a perimeter of the city. But business development slowed because there were so many other markets opening up. Why go to Hartford? What Hartford— When we started to get more active with urban design is when the market was coming back. People really wanted to be there because it was a great opportunity to build ten stories or more of development. To maximize the use of the space, make more use of it, make more money. I mean, that was the whole concept. That's what That's what development's all about. Where had the field gone by then in urban planning? Because now it had gone through urban renewal. It had gone through model cities. It had gone through cities. It had gone through all of these, like, grand federal visions. The model cities and a lot of the social focuses of planning at the time in some ways created a shadow government in that people in neighborhoods were invested with certain powers to meet and discuss. But they were not always welcomed at the town hall, to the city hall, to present or to— There was a gap. It was expectations, great society expectations. And got people—you could say if they encouraged those people to go to town hall and demand certain things, they might have gotten better results. It would take less time anyway. It would not necessitate so many meetings in their own community to do so many things. That was a perception. And I think there's something to that. You know, the that. You know, the rubber hits the road at the town council. And they're not—no town council has got all the answers to everything. But it's basically a resource allocation function. How much you got to spend, how much you need to spend, what have you got to innovate. And in those years, there was tremendous amounts of money to innovate because because the federal government was parceling it out at tremendous rates. So you sometimes got yourself involved in projects that you— because you can get the money that you may have not had any chance of actually implementing. Is it so— And urban renewal was one of those. Let me just finish the thought. So So are you saying—I'm just trying to clarify. Are Are you saying that because you're trying to qualify for federal funding as the only way to get money in, there's this like constant pressure to keep it moving and keep innovating and keep going along with whatever the next program is out of the federal government? Well, I think that's probably true. It supported your staffing. It supported the improvement of your city if you did these things. And there's a lot of different things it could point to that made it work. It was small in comparison. You know, there was really—everybody's running around trying to do things. I mean, with redevelopment, for example, tremendous amounts of money taken to purchase properties, relocate tenants, clear buildings and put in improvements. And it's sick because by the time you got to that phase, you already missed your market or whatever that market was. There was never a clear indication of, you know, how much fully the market needed what you were selling. Many of them were socially desirable. So it became a real problem. It took a lot of staff time, took a lot of money, didn't show a lot of benefit. It took years. As far as some of the other programs, maybe you created— it did encourage more people, local people, to come up to speed and volunteer and be part of their community. Citizen participation was a big deal. However, like today, it's not much different in the sense that you can go and rant, but you don't necessarily have to be responsible for what for what you're talking about, which has always been an issue. But you just said that the people who were participating in Model Cities, for example, should have gone to Town Hall to rant. Yes, yes. No, they should. What I'm saying is that there was a whole lot of other entities created during during the time who needed or used the same funding resources that would have otherwise gone to town, to city. So there was sort of a push-pull there in a way. But it's very frustrating. I I mean, Hartford, over the years since we were trying to bring it to a point, it's taken so long. And I don't think it's because of the absence of plans. No. It's, I think, an absence of stick-to-itiveness. You know, you do what you can do and don't take on something until until you finish it kind of thing. Is the federal government tone-deaf? They are these days. Have you ever heard anything about the housing and urban development these days? It was in the forefront every day in the newspaper back in those days. Something was going on. I don't know if they do anything anymore. But it was in the forefront, but yet what I'm taking away from most of what you've said is that the vision and the ideas behind it, like there's all this grand social vision that's informing these programs, but they're not economically feasible in the long term. Or they take so long, they're not economically feasible? They take economically feasible? They take a long time. I mean, one example, I was working with the San Juan Center. And we took a trip down to Florida, a couple of places, a couple of places couple of places, a couple of places to look at Mercados, a little neighborhood shopping, for lack of a better term, mall, because it's what it is, all one building. And that was the 1970s. Well, they just finished one in Parkville, finally. I mean, it takes a long time between you get these ideas. That's where the frustration comes in. It's just maybe the ideas are too radical. Maybe they require too much public money because the marketplace hasn't really blessed them yet. Is that market, is Parkville considered thriving, successful? I think so. It's one of the highlights of the community. Economically, how is it doing? I think it's doing well. I don't know for sure. I don't play there anymore. I keep reading about it, and they're expanding more tenants in there. And it sort of looks like the market in Philadelphia, an old train station. It creates energy. It It creates something in the community that doesn't exist or didn't exist. Otherwise, what it is is it tends to give a personality to a project other than just taking the next stop and shop that comes out. There's people, there's owners involved. There's people who have their lives involved. And that, to me, is a wonderful thing. It's something that's diminished over COVID. A A lot of these small shops went out of business. Those small shops are what we rely on to create the character of a community. So while I was planning in Hartford, I was saying, I think we need to subsidize the first floor levels. The office buildings built on these properties had ridiculous rents. The rents then were probably twice what they are. The rents then were twice as much as they are today because they overestimated. But at the first floor level, they took out a lot of the mom and pops because they couldn't afford a new building. That was my feeling, that we need to subsidize to create that energy. The worst thing you could put on the first floor would be a bank. It's closed after three. It's deadly. There's no public activity. So from an urban design standpoint, that's where I was creating a list of bonuses. If you do this, we'll give you that. You need more space. So it's a way of creating a downtown that's active and vital. It has the public amenities that can help attract. Now, you're seeing a lot of conversion of office space to residential now. Well, of course, office space is the most available in our economy because the vacancy rate is so high after COVID. It may never come back. So you're seeing these housing projects, not because necessarily those people wanted to do it, but what are they going to do with their buildings? They've got a big investment there that's not being used. So the market is working in that sense. I see. What about all of the ugly buildings that was right at the corridor of Dunkin' Donuts Park? That redevelopment. So now San Juan Center is back in the tanks. Yeah, that's sad. It's It's sad in a way because there's some beautiful buildings. Of course, they've been abused over the years. So there really wasn't enough to make it worthwhile. I always liked the Flatiron Building where the Arrowhead Cafe was. That's a new concept, and it in part will be helped certainly by the ballpark, but better if they do succeed in covering over Route 84 and reconnecting. I mean, the whole story of highways, how they cut through Hartford, how they took the community away from the river, how it separated communities. I don't know all the information behind that. All All I do know is that G. Fox and Company was a major player back then when the highways were built, and they wanted people to come to their store. Can't fault them for that. So the San Juan Center is planning housing. Yeah, they've been doing that for years. They They had a lot of small projects. So they're trying to have folks age in place because people want to stay in the city when they get older. They don't want to have to leave. I don't know. I think not that they—now these projects are getting bigger. In the days I was working with San Juan, they were dealing individual buildings. You get a certain amount of money, but put a little group together that that can retrofit them and get them back online. This is a much bigger undertaking. I don't know how San Juan Center is playing it except to be a local supporter. I can't imagine they have the depth to deal with that. I'm sure they're a smaller operation than they were when I was there. But they're there because they are the so-called neighborhood organization. They've been there for years. Right, and it certainly wouldn't look right if all of this development passed them by. I mean, the park came and it's taken a while for it to benefit them, but it eventually is now benefiting them. Now folks are interested in that corridor and in Sacred Heart Church as a museum, potentially. It all gets back to creating market attraction. After I left Hartford, I spent five years doing commercial real estate appraisal. I understood then better as to what the motivations might be and where they might be better suited for investment. What brought you to real estate after urban planning? I left Hartford after 18 years there. I said, I don't want to do this again. I was frustrated by the fact that planning, while it's a noble profession and certainly very important for how we as a society continue to function or not, was never really quite adopted or adapted. Politicians who run every two years, three years, whatever it was, they're not interested in something that's going to take 10 or 15 years. They can't commit themselves. They can't commit themselves to establish the priorities. They say, well, we think we should do these things for the next five years and then we'll see where we're at for them. These are some options after that. But it became the supermarket. The cities and towns became supermarkets. Everybody's whims and needs. Not that that's wrong, but again, it's a resource allocation model. There's only so much money to go around. There's only a certain number of things you can do, whether it's money or manpower or both. Those are the decisions that people elected to office should be making. But they don't. I said, okay, I really gained a lot. I did a lot while I was in Hartford. I have a lot to be proud of. Do you want to go to another city and do this all over again? I was thrilled about it. this all over I was thrilled about it. Just to play that same frustration. It was on a hiatus. I took five years with a friend who just started this appraisal. He broke away and started his own company. There were a bunch of us that were working there. My frustration there was I'm making a lot of money for somebody else. I'm not interested in the money. I'm interested in the result and doing the right thing. That's when Sharon and I started a garden design business. It sounds totally different, but it wasn't. It's still site planning. It had to do with organizing and priorities and resources and the same kind of modeling while she was mostly doing horticultural work. I don't know. Today, it's a difficult challenge for urban planners to get their ideas across. You look at the town of Bloomfield. I worked on the town plans for the last 20 years. I thought they were quite well. In one case, I was a consultant for the economic development portion in 2000 and a resident in 2010. But who reads that after it's finished? Who reads it? Who looks at the implementation? Not even the planning people try and make it work. It's like, okay, that's done. Now, it's back to work as usual. Why is it so hard to get traction? I don't know. All those ideas were honestly and forthrightly prepared and developed. It made sense at the time. In this state, the plan is, again, it's only advisory. There's nobody. The council doesn't have to approve it, so they don't have to read it. I came to the conclusion that if it wasn't for the state of Connecticut mandating that every 10 years you have to update your plan, there'd be no planning. What would exist then? I don't know. It would just be like what it is now. It was one of the frustrating things when I got on council, trying to see where is the implementation of our plan? What are you doing for it? The staff wasn't even clued in. Can you talk about your transition to living in Bloomfield? I came from Cambridge to take this job in Hartford. We had a couple of days to find a place. There wasn't a lot of vacancy in Hartford. I was looking mostly up in the Blue Hills area neighborhood. It took a while. I said, are we ready to buy a house? I'm not sure. So we rented in Rocky Hill for a year. Then when it was time to move, I shouldn't say what it was time for, then we're thinking, okay, this was only temporary. So I went to look to buy a house, and Bloomfield was attractive. I I didn't have to live in Hartford at the time. There was no requirements. And I didn't find what I was looking for. What was attractive about Bloomfield? I mean, say, like Windsor, South Windsor, Manchester. Okay. I had heard of it before. An uncle who lived in Hartford, an uncle in Terrace, moved to Bloomfield, became very active with school desegregation, those kinds of things, or integration. They had good things to say about it. Again, a place I had been to before. How long before you moved to Bloomfield did your uncle move to Bloomfield? Oh, he was gone. They were already gone. He gone. He was living in West Hartford by then. But then. But Bloomfield, I found a house that sort of suited me. The house I'm in now, this old house, was what I really was looking for in Rocky Hill. I I found one, but I said to myself, I'm not sure I'm ready to own a house that old and deal with it until I go to school. So I bought a house, a nice six-room ranch on Crestview Drive, and went to school in that house and added a few rooms in the basement and and did a lot of renovations and things that I could handle at the time because because it was in good shape. Then after 10 years I said, okay, I want environment. And we found this place. How did you find this house? Sharon was taking a yoga class or something with somebody else who was a realtor and and said, I've got a house that's coming on the market. So before it was on the market, we were up here talking to the owners. A very nice couple. He was a professor. He had taught in someplace in the Middle East. He said one night Henry Kissinger was here. Kissinger was involved with some international studies. So anyway, we fell in love with the place and and we made a lot of changes to the insides and so forth after many years of living here to figure it out. This house is on the stagecoach route from Westfield, Madison to Hartford, which ones are in the front door. That's why the house faces the woods there. There are still iron bars on the basement windows because the sheriffs used to overnight prisoners in the basement. These are little bits and pieces of Bloomfield. You're You're not thinking about it, are you? But we went from a standard six-room ranch to a house with high ceilings. Wow, that's pretty elegant. What did you do to change the house? What did you do to the interior? It was a bathroom with a door that blocked everything. The kitchen was kind of old-fashioned, dark. Things just piled out in front of the windows, cabinets and such. We would sit here, which was the toilet, and look out the one window and said, you know, the deck would be nice, a nice view down the garden. You see the stairs right from there. We took one of these windows from the next room over, so we put two together and put a big French door opening out in the other room to create more light, more visibility. We did this kitchen over. We spent a lot of time thinking about it. That's one of the good things and bad things because when you spend a lot of time doing things, you sometimes undo them, which is probably where we're at now. Bloomfield had no sense of what Bloomfield was like. I hadn't done a lot of research. Again, I'd been here. It looked nice, but I'd come up to different parts of town and you'd see a place like this, which is almost like being in New Hampshire. We spent those times in the center of town raising kids, had babies, going to PTA meetings and stuff like that and being part of the community that way. Met some nice people. Then I got involved in being on committees, fighting. The first one was the Community Awareness Task Force, which was set up to fight discrimination, particularly in the real estate industry at the time, which was pretty much redlining Bloomfield. Just got to meet some nice people and we're still here. It's 45, 55 years later. Did you feel like you were following in your uncle's footsteps when when you got involved in the movement? To some extent. To some To some extent. I mean, it's I mean, it's part of your background. I'm sure it was my aunt's motivation. She She was a communist from way back and swore like a sailor, whereas he was mostly in business and did very well in business in Hartford and became nationally involved during the Civil Rights Movement. He and Martin Luther King met several times and they had him in his home. Used to supply the Southern Christian Leadership Conference with cars for election registration and so forth. But it was a family that put their money where their mouth was and and they lived it and that was good enough for me. I think our whole family was sort of that way. I don't recall anybody in the family that, again, I think that's part of being Jewish too. You've been through a lot. You know that people are pretty much alike. Throughout the ages, you constantly empathize with those that get the same kind of treatment that your people got. Before the Black Power Movement, I think the strongest support for social justice was from the Jewish community working with blacks. What was the Jewish community like in Bloomfield specifically, You know, I don't know for sure. We didn't really participate much in the synagogue life, but there were a lot of them. There were a lot of synagogues at that time? Yeah, there were one, two, maybe three. Now there are none. From my work in Hartford, we helped to start what they call Chavura, which is C-H-A-V-U-R-A. I forgot the black. That's pretty close. Which is people starting their own religious experience. And it was more inclusive. Because primarily in Hartford in the West End, there were a lot of mixed families. And we wanted to acknowledge that. We weren't ready to go back to the strict synagogue life. It wasn't that great when I was growing up, but it was something we had to do. I never really got tuned into it. But I miss it now, the sense of community provided. Because you'd see all the people you needed to see at any time. So why is there a migration of Jewish folks out of Bloomfield to the point where there are no more synagogues? And where are they going? Is it Hartford? I think they aged out. North end of Hartford was very heavily Jewish. And when the suburbs opened up, which is both a market thing and probably, I don't know about West Hartford, this area, but downstate along the coast, there was a lot of deed restrictions. Who could live there? Different places. So I think the natural movement was out of Hartford, just like it is today, moving north and moving west. The Italians moved south to Wethersfield, where their people were. Now I think that population has aged out. And since the 70s, 80s, people are all over the place. They're all over the country, all over the world. My closest relative now is Northampton, a cousin. The next closest one might be New Jersey. Some are in Texas, Wyoming, all over the world. So when that happens, you go to a place where you just have to take care of your own needs. A lot of people just probably moved down to Florida or other places because they aged out. Younger kids don't have attachments of that sort anymore. So what does Bloomfield look like? Is that what's going to keep people rooted here? Or is this process of aging out going to keep happening to other groups? Well, no, that's a good question for a planner. Because obviously there aren't enough people in the process to continue this movement of migration that we've had. And because of the effect on the planet, we can't afford to do things like that anymore. It's more likely that there'll be more of a creating more density rather than using up the little less density areas that we have. I think the future is a redevelopment, not new development, for sustainable reasons, which will come at a good time, I think. It'll come at a time when people have more or less begun to feel better with each other. That's my hope. New York has become pretty good in that sense. You have people from all different areas of the world and different types. They get along pretty well. I think that's likely to be the future. I don't see further migrations. I I think the population in Bloomfield now will be pretty much the same numbers. I shouldn't say same numbers because we're adding more apartment houses. But the same people. Their families will stay. People will come from around the area or other places. But But it's not going to be in mass numbers like we saw in the past. Were the planners prepared for Bloomfield to grow the way that it did and become as diverse as it did? I think that was very much intended. That's the way we wanted it to be. That was the motivation back then. That's one of the things that made us become an all-American city was the intent that we wanted to make it happen here. What was going on with you when Bloomfield was the all-American city? I was working in Hartford but I'm a resident and being involved with trying to fight redlining and blockbusting in the town. But I think the all-American city motivation came more from my uncle's generation. They started that process. You know, you can have a title but it requires a good deal of attention to keep it. And there's a lot of different ways that being an all-American city can be interpreted. And I think by and large there's a strong, a good sense of community here in Bloomfield, different-- throughout throughout the community. My one sense is that there's a large part of the population whose community still revolves totally around the church. Just churches in general, whatever denomination? Whatever, whatever. And while that's salvation for many, historically, it's the public space, it's the community as you get out and meet other people and be part of a community that makes it more fun, makes it more real and prolongs the dream, prolongs the all-American city concept. So it takes work, it takes work. Which kind of public spaces? Any public space. Any public space? Sidewalks. You know, you walk around and meet somebody, you know somebody. Where'd Where'd you meet them? Because I saw them over at some other activity or something. But then again, here's the problem. The problem is we're going through a time frame in which everything's changing over time. So what your expectations were and your mindset was 30 years ago is ago is totally different now as people have totally tuned into their phones and AI. You know, so there's always something else that changes that makes plans, that helps to make plans ineffective or unattainable. But ultimately people want a good place to live and a place to raise their kids. Yes. kids. Yes. Yes. And I say, you get those hummingbirds coming through the window? Oh. So yeah, exactly. That's all that people ask. And I think this town does it as well as any other. I got frustrated being on council because I just couldn't see people moving in the same direction. They all say the same things but they act differently. I don't know, I was just frustrated. I couldn't take it any longer. You can check any longer. You can check that out. I don't out. I don't care. Put that in or not. Is the town supportive of all the apartment housing that's going up? Well, the town is supportive. The initial ones that went in there, which probably at least 10 years from start to where it is now, were assisted with a tax break because people felt A, that we needed to create more energy in the center of town, but B, that we're no longer a farm community and we can't continue to think that we're going to be low density when Hartford is the center and we can't continue to spread out as far as we can, that along these transit routes are going to have to be more density and that we may not be able to live like we live today in another 30, 40 years. That's frightening to some people. Of course it is because they don't know anything else. People in New York City have been living that way for how long? That's another density in consideration. But not all of New York is 70-story buildings. I think the average height is four stories. It's not much different than Hartford. But that's the difficult thing in trying to anticipate the future. I think we did it pretty well in the 50s. There was a lot of thought. There was a lot of planning. The big investment in highways and infrastructure led to a tremendous expansion. A lot of that highway infrastructure hasn't been maintained or have the rail lines and other kinds of infrastructure. Now we're 60 years later and we have to reinvent all that stuff. So putting on your planning cap, what would be your last plan? The last time you plan for Bloomfield. If you could be as pragmatic and as visionary as possible, what would that plan look like? It depends. I think we're still on the right track. I would like to eliminate a lot of the commercial structures that require our own parking lots, especially in the town center because it's too much of a detraction and increase the density there, which we are doing, I think almost 425 new apartments in the town center has been a source of revitalization and energy for some of the poor commercial entities that had to go through the pandemic. Some had to close. But I think it's going to be a strong buttress for future growth, provided that growth is properly managed. To me, I think that means, again, creating spaces for humans to meet each other, public spaces, whether it's between the curb and the store entry. You don't want to break up. We've struggled to deal with a 1970s version of what the town should look like and the Wittenberry Mall. Both plans since then have said, okay, bring that out to the sidewalk on Park Avenue. Make yourself a 15-foot sidewalk, places for chairs, sit, benches, where people walk and can actually meet somebody else, sit down and have a conversation. We're blessed with a lot of natural space. It gives a relief to all of that. But every time you go someplace, you've got to get in your car and go to the next door, instead of thinking of walking. People walk when there's something of interest to see. Some people walk for exercise, but not too many people who are going shopping are walking for exercise. This is something I picked up in Hartford. If you have to walk two blocks to get to where you're going and there's nothing visually attractive or something to see in between, you're less likely to do that. to see in between, you're less likely to do that. So you have to create that energy, and that energy is both physical, it's visual, it's visual, and it's social. It's a social place. That's the reason people come to places where there can be gathering. We're fortunate to have a small town center, but it's not arranged like any other. It's a farm town center. It may have been at one time, but I think that the apartment houses, while located, will do a good job. There's no reason for necessarily to have to spread too far out. We have to also think about, what's our goal in terms of our future population? We've been pretty flat-lined for 20 years. Connecticut is a slow-growth state. In a way, that's good. It makes it easier to plan. So with that in mind, you can make the decisions about where new growth should go, but I think new growth should go where old growth was, just from a purely environmental standpoint, and also because the old growth was in the community, it was closer in. That would be some of the planning issues I would be concerned about. People do feel like they can age in place here. They're here. They're not necessarily escaping to Florida. Well, we have two very wonderful retirement communities. Obviously, they're not necessarily affordable for everybody. Right, I don't know how many of those people have places in Florida. I'm sure some do. But Duncaster was created to keep folks here. Yeah. here. Yeah. Instead of— Both of— Both of them. Is that sustainable? Is it sustainable? Well, I think it's sustainable. It's going to prove to be sustainable, because I'm not sure anything in Florida is going to be sustainable after a while. And they're expanding, right? They They expanded in Seabury quite a bit. Yeah. You know, when you get older, you do have an issue with the cold weather. It's just natural. But to go to a place like Florida— My parents went there in the 70s, and they had a condo on West Palm Beach, and we'd go down to visit. I didn't like the place. The condos were nice, but the area was all highway, and and shopping centers, flat, and traffic. Traffic grew and grew and grew and grew. It It was amazing. But you see, only in five or six years, what it became. The only thing that has attractive to it is the warm weather, golf, and fishing. If you do any of those things, you've got something going. You're in heaven, yeah. But everything in between gets to be a chore. But now that climate change is happening, maybe we'll be in Florida after a while. Who knows? If we should live so long. And your children have spread out where? Yeah, my son lives in Salt Spring, an island off the coast of Vancouver Island in Canada, British Columbia. My daughter lives on an island upstairs. So we're— And your brother is? He's in New Jersey. He's in New Jersey, but spends time elsewhere? Yeah. How often do you see him? Twice a year. And do your children tell the same story about sort of their idyllic childhood in the little town of Bloomfield, sort of the way that you grew up in Patterson? Oh, that's a good question. I can't answer that, but I can tell you that my daughter went— took, I think, her sophomore year or junior year, she went to Watkinson School because of the arts program there. And she didn't last—she did good in the art, but she said, I want to get back to my friends in Bloomfield. You know, you grow up in a town, you know everybody. It's different. So they both loved living here. They both loved being in the— I hate to use the word because you hackneyed after all, diverse community. But the benefits of that kind of living, just like I found in college, were exceptional. People are people. That's what, you know, that's the difference. That's all you got to do. It's all you need to know. It has nothing to know. It has nothing to do with color. You know, when I got to travel years later, on the mountaintop somewhere near the Andes, near Machu Picchu, and there's these locals sitting on the hillside making their wares, and four o'clock happens and a woman reaches into her pocket, picks up her cell phone and says, okay, we've got to go. You know, life is life. It happens everywhere. We are not exclusive in how we live. Everybody has the benefits of technology to a great extent and are just trying to survive as much as you are, or anybody else's. I do wish we lived in a world where people think diversity is so commonplace that it becomes hackneyed. I hope that becomes the reality. Yeah. Well, I just can't. I don't see it any other way. I don't see it. Again, my life experience brought me to that point. When you were working on these kinds of issues in Bloomfield, did you feel like, was there a particular point when you felt like you were victorious, or maybe that's too far? You were relatively successful in accomplishing the goals that you had. I ask because other folks have said, you know, there were some victories, but then some folks decided, yeah, we're yeah, we're moving to Avon and Simsbury. Yeah, well, the victories for me probably don't necessarily equate to victories for others, what other people look for in their life. In 1989, when our daughter was in high school, we went to PTA meetings, and one meeting, a young girl stands up and says, I can't wait to get out of the school at the end of the day. It's so boring. Surprising, this turned out to be Joe Suggs' daughter. And so Sharon and I said, that's not good. Let's do something about it. The kids are bored? What are you going to do? Well, we had experience working in Hartford. We helped start a theater company over there. We were board members of Peace Train, which was organized to give kids in neighborhoods an opportunity to hear live music and see movies. Eventually, its claim to fame was the New England Fiddle Contest, which ran for over 30 years in Bushnell Park. But it gave us a feeling that there's nothing we can't do. We can put on events. So we went to the principal of the high school and said, listen, here's what we heard from some of your students, from one of your students, a very important student of yours, and we want to make this school a little bit more fun, more relevant. So we'd like to take over the school for a day, I can imagine today you'd say something like that. In the morning, we would propose holding seminars, maybe 8 or 10 different seminars for the kids, things like Is There Life After Death, Sex, Drugs, and Rock and Roll, Snakes and Herpetology, Don't Get Caught With Drugs, Taught by Prisoners from Summers, a whole bunch of things. In the afternoon, we proposed to have field events between the classes and the teachers, put on a field team as well, and one of them will win at the end of the day, and and we're going to call this Spirit Day. Well, people were saying, oh, the kids are going to come back a day off, they're not going to show up, and if they do, they're going to be running in the halls being stupid. Well, they all showed up, no one was in the halls. It was a great day, and at the end of the day, I don't know if I was wiping their brow, but wiping their brow, but by the time we get everybody back in the gymnasium, there'd the gymnasium, there'd be riots. Well, it never happened that way, because kids are good kids, kids do something for kids they appreciate. When they see appreciate. When they see something different, they're not necessarily going to run away from it. Not that it was away from it. Not that it was the greatest thing since sliced bread, but bread, but it offered something different, that kids need different, that kids need that. So we put that on. Next year, the Board of Ed said, well, I think we did it two years, and then the Board took it over, and they did nothing with it. It showed us that you can do things, you can make a change. Like I said, I don't know if you can do those things today, it would be too much ritual, too much involved. So we did that, we knew we could change things. So when I was on council, two things I thought were very important. One was to provide some sort of funding mechanism for development that the town could use as an incentive. could use as an incentive. So other towns had been using something called tax increment financing, I put a package together, tried and got it organized, created created an ordinance, had discussions over it. The whole idea being that, from my experience of redevelopment, from the day you have the idea, the day the idea, the day something comes in, it's totally different than what you expected, and you've lost so much time, and it costs you so much more money. Why go through the process of buying up, maintaining, holding on, until something comes along. You've got somebody who's interested, work with that person, you're already four steps ahead of the game. So they're still fooling around ahead of the game. So they're still fooling around with it, it may be something going to be used on Toby Road, perhaps using that, but it was designed for specific areas, town town center, Blue Hills, areas that are commercial corridors mostly. And the other thing I did was what I did in Hartford, I created a design review board. In Bloomfield, especially in the town center, there are design criteria for what goes in, not necessarily, it's not architectural, it's context, it's connections, it's massing, it's what we call urban design, the elements of the community, and movement of cars and people, blah, blah, blah, blah, all that stuff. So I created a design review board because nobody was really able or was looking at the plans that were being presented. When the heirloom flats came in, that first apartment building, no one questioned it. And yet you had this whole ordinance that said here are the design guidelines for your area. Why is it looking like that? It's totally foreign. So we, you know, managed to work with the developer on the subsequent phases. He's thrilled, and I think the town is thrilled because it's going to have more contextual relationship. So it doesn't look like a mishmash. And also, you know, at least it can be better connected to the public spaces. So it was just, it's not, you know, in terms of world you know, in terms of world achievements, it's not the greatest, but at least it filled a gap, and I was happy about that. It sounds like it's been a good life. I'm very happy. You know, I look back, and again, I compare myself, as I always had to, to my brother's experience. And he'll be the first to tell you, he envies me. So we both have a good laugh over some of these things, but I figure we made it this far, and, you know, we did what we had to do. We knew when to put some nuts away and grow them so that we're not, you know, indigent at this point in our life so far. We worked hard, had directions, had some directions, not directions like someone else gave them, but them, but also, I I cannot say it, I cannot discount the fact that that we probably were living during the best time of this country's life. Best time economically? Everything, post-war. Opportunity, tremendous. Again, I know that some of those opportunities were not shared with all the people over time, and, you know, there's a lot of reasons for that, the least of which are some of the people who purport to say they're doing the best for people. My religion's better than yours. Anyway. Well, thank you so much for taking the time. I'm glad I the time. I'm glad I got to... I think it went better the first time. When I wasn't expecting it. Why? Did you hold yourself back? No, no, no, it just seemed to flow. Yeah, it flowed.

story.description: The interview with Mr. David Mann offers a reflective exploration of his life, touching on several key themes and experiences. It includes discussions about his Jewish family background, experiences growing up in Patterson and transitioning to suburban life in Fairlawn, the significance of familial and cultural identity, and his professional journey in urban planning. Mr. Mann's narrative also covers urban development challenges, such as those regarding Hartford and Bloomfield, the evolution of urban planning, and the social dynamics of historical and modern communities. It reflects on themes of suburban migration, community cohesion, and intergenerational family impacts, offering insights into the socio-economic transformation of American cities in the post-war era.
story.title: David Mann oral history.WAV
